note: Exam-style APPLICATION / transfer items (PRD 4.1 PerformanceItem, 7.2, reviewer push). These are scenario/reasoning questions that apply a topic's idea in new words — NOT 'what is X' recall. Mastery gates on accuracy here, so clearing a topic means you can USE it, the way the MCAT tests. 'paraphrase_of' marks the topic idea it re-tests, powering the paraphrase gap (memory vs application). Hand-authored to cover every topic in data/mcat_outline.json (~2 items/topic).

mcat::bio_biochem::amino_acids.stem: A peptide is placed in a buffer at pH 1. A glutamate side chain (pKa ≈ 4.1) will most likely be:
A researcher needs the one amino acid that will NOT introduce a stereocenter at its alpha carbon. They should choose:
A membrane-spanning stretch of a protein sits in the lipid bilayer's oily core. It is most likely enriched in:
At a pH exactly equal to an amino acid's isoelectric point (pI), the molecule carries:
mcat::bio_biochem::amino_acids.paraphrase_of: net charge is zero at the pI

mcat::bio_biochem::protein_structure.stem: Adding a reducing agent that breaks disulfide bonds unfolds a protein, and removing it lets the protein refold and regain function. The disrupted bonds mainly stabilized which level of structure?
A single point mutation swaps a surface glutamate for valine, and the protein now aggregates through hydrophobic contacts (as in sickle-cell hemoglobin). This is best explained as:
choices: A primary-sequence change that creates a new hydrophobic patch altering higher-order structure
A change affecting only secondary structure
mcat::bio_biochem::protein_structure.paraphrase_of: primary sequence change drives higher-order structure

mcat::bio_biochem::protein_structure.stem: Heating an enzyme far above its optimal temperature destroys its activity, yet its peptide bonds stay intact. The heat most directly disrupted:
choices: The noncovalent interactions holding the 3-D fold

mcat::bio_biochem::protein_structure.stem: Hemoglobin's cooperative oxygen binding depends on interactions among its four separate polypeptide subunits. This requires which level of structure?

mcat::bio_biochem::enzymes.stem: A drug slows an enzyme, but adding more substrate fully restores the original Vmax. On a Lineweaver–Burk plot the drug's line, versus control, will:
An inhibitor lowers an enzyme's Vmax and cannot be overcome by more substrate. This inhibitor most likely binds:
Enzyme A has Km = 0.1 mM and enzyme B has Km = 5 mM for the same substrate. Compared with B, enzyme A has:
mcat::bio_biochem::enzymes.paraphrase_of: low Km means high substrate affinity

mcat::bio_biochem::enzymes.stem: A stomach protease works best at pH 2 but loses activity in the small intestine at pH 8. The most likely reason is that:
choices: Its active-site shape depends on pH
mcat::bio_biochem::enzymes.paraphrase_of: enzyme activity depends on optimal pH

mcat::bio_biochem::carbohydrates.stem: Humans digest starch (α-1,4 links) but not cellulose, though both are glucose polymers. Human enzymes cannot hydrolyze:
Glucose and galactose have identical formulas and differ only in the configuration at a single chiral carbon. They are best described as:
mcat::bio_biochem::carbohydrates.paraphrase_of: epimers differ at one chiral center

mcat::bio_biochem::carbohydrates.stem: The body stores glucose for rapid mobilization mainly in the form of:
mcat::bio_biochem::carbohydrates.paraphrase_of: glycogen is the animal glucose store

mcat::bio_biochem::carbohydrates.stem: α- and β-glucose differ only in the configuration at the new stereocenter formed when the ring closes (the anomeric carbon). They are:
mcat::bio_biochem::carbohydrates.paraphrase_of: anomers differ at the anomeric carbon

mcat::bio_biochem::lipids.stem: Phospholipids dropped into water spontaneously form a bilayer with tails inward. The main thermodynamic driver is:
choices: A decrease in the system's total entropy

mcat::bio_biochem::lipids.stem: Replacing a membrane's saturated phospholipid tails with tails containing cis double bonds will most likely make the membrane, at a given temperature:
Which vitamin is most likely absorbed along with dietary fat and stored in the body's fat tissue?
Soap lifts grease from a plate because each soap molecule has:
choices: A hydrophilic head and a hydrophobic tail

mcat::bio_biochem::nucleic_acids.stem: Two DNA samples are heated until the strands separate. The sample that requires a higher temperature to melt most likely has:
mcat::bio_biochem::nucleic_acids.paraphrase_of: GC content raises DNA melting temperature

mcat::bio_biochem::nucleic_acids.stem: One DNA strand reads 5'-ATGC-3'. Read in the 5'→3' direction, its complementary strand is:
In a sample of double-stranded DNA, 30% of the bases are adenine. The percentage that are cytosine is about:
The two strands of a DNA duplex are held to each other by ___, while the sugar-phosphate backbone within one strand is held together by ___:
mcat::bio_biochem::nucleic_acids.paraphrase_of: H-bonds pair strands; covalent bonds form the backbone

mcat::bio_biochem::dna_replication.stem: DNA polymerase synthesizes only 5'→3'. On the lagging strand this constraint forces:
choices: Short Okazaki fragments later sealed by ligase
mcat::bio_biochem::dna_replication.paraphrase_of: lagging strand made in Okazaki fragments

mcat::bio_biochem::dna_replication.stem: A drug lets the replication fork open but replication stalls from unrelieved supercoiling tension ahead of it. The drug most likely inhibits:
After one round of replication, each daughter DNA molecule has one original strand and one newly made strand. Replication is therefore:
DNA polymerase cannot begin a new strand from nothing; it needs an existing 3'-OH to extend. That starting 3'-OH is provided by:
mcat::bio_biochem::dna_replication.paraphrase_of: RNA primer supplies the 3'-OH for polymerase

mcat::bio_biochem::transcription_transla.stem: A mutation changes a codon from GAA to GAG, yet the same amino acid (Glu) is still inserted. This is possible because:
choices: Each tRNA reads exactly one codon
The change fell in an intron
mcat::bio_biochem::transcription_transla.paraphrase_of: degeneracy of the genetic code (silent mutation)

mcat::bio_biochem::transcription_transla.stem: A single-nucleotide insertion early in a gene usually has a more severe effect than a single-nucleotide substitution because it:
A point mutation turns an internal codon into a premature stop codon. The resulting protein is most likely:
As the ribosome translates an mRNA, it builds the polypeptide beginning at:
choices: The 5' end, adding from the N-terminus

mcat::bio_biochem::gene_regulation.stem: In the lac operon, adding glucose lowers cAMP, weakening CAP binding, so transcription falls even when lactose is present. Glucose therefore acts through:
choices: Positive control via the CAP–cAMP complex

mcat::bio_biochem::gene_regulation.stem: A transcription factor boosts expression of a gene located thousands of base pairs away. This is best explained by the factor:
choices: Binding an enhancer and looping the DNA to the promoter
mcat::bio_biochem::gene_regulation.paraphrase_of: enhancers act at a distance via DNA looping

mcat::bio_biochem::gene_regulation.stem: Heavy methylation of a gene's promoter region is most commonly associated with:
In the lac operon with no lactose present, the lac repressor binds the operator and:
choices: Boosts transcription of the lac genes
Blocks transcription of the lac genes
mcat::bio_biochem::gene_regulation.paraphrase_of: repressor blocks the operator without inducer

mcat::bio_biochem::glycolysis.stem: In vigorously exercising muscle with little oxygen, glycolysis continues because NADH is reoxidized to NAD⁺ by:
A cell with abundant ATP down-regulates glucose breakdown mainly by inhibiting:
The NET ATP produced by substrate-level phosphorylation from one glucose through glycolysis is:
Unlike the citric acid cycle, glycolysis takes place in the:

mcat::bio_biochem::citric_acid_cycle.stem: Per acetyl-CoA entering it, the citric acid cycle's main DIRECT products are:
choices: A large amount of ATP directly
mcat::bio_biochem::citric_acid_cycle.paraphrase_of: TCA yields reduced carriers, little direct ATP

mcat::bio_biochem::citric_acid_cycle.stem: When the electron transport chain is blocked, the citric acid cycle also slows sharply because:
choices: NAD⁺ and FAD are no longer regenerated
mcat::bio_biochem::citric_acid_cycle.paraphrase_of: TCA depends on ETC to regenerate NAD+/FAD

mcat::bio_biochem::citric_acid_cycle.stem: In a eukaryotic cell, the citric acid cycle operates in the:
mcat::bio_biochem::citric_acid_cycle.paraphrase_of: TCA runs in the mitochondrial matrix

mcat::bio_biochem::citric_acid_cycle.stem: The carbon atoms released as CO2 during the citric acid cycle originate from:
mcat::bio_biochem::citric_acid_cycle.paraphrase_of: TCA exhales carbons that came from acetyl-CoA

mcat::bio_biochem::oxidative_phosphoryla.stem: A toxin makes the inner mitochondrial membrane leaky to protons. The immediate effect is:
choices: Continued electron transport but little ATP; energy lost as heat

mcat::bio_biochem::oxidative_phosphoryla.stem: Cyanide blocks Complex IV of the electron transport chain. The immediate consequence is that:
choices: Electron flow halts, the proton gradient collapses, and ATP synthesis stops
Only heat output rises while ATP is unchanged
The citric acid cycle speeds up
mcat::bio_biochem::oxidative_phosphoryla.paraphrase_of: ETC inhibition stops oxidative ATP synthesis

mcat::bio_biochem::oxidative_phosphoryla.stem: In oxidative phosphorylation, molecular oxygen functions as:
mcat::bio_biochem::oxidative_phosphoryla.paraphrase_of: O2 is the terminal electron acceptor

mcat::bio_biochem::oxidative_phosphoryla.stem: When one glucose is fully oxidized aerobically, the largest share of ATP is generated during:

mcat::bio_biochem::cell_biology.stem: A red blood cell is placed in a hypertonic solution. Water will:
choices: Move into the cell, making it swell
Move out of the cell, making it shrink
Move only if ATP is spent

mcat::bio_biochem::cell_biology.stem: A protein destined to be secreted from the cell is synthesized on ribosomes that are:
choices: Bound to the rough endoplasmic reticulum
mcat::bio_biochem::cell_biology.paraphrase_of: secretory proteins made on rough ER

mcat::bio_biochem::cell_biology.stem: A cell type with very high ATP demand (such as cardiac muscle) is most likely to contain an unusually large number of:
Moving ions across a membrane AGAINST their concentration gradient requires:
mcat::bio_biochem::cell_biology.paraphrase_of: active transport uses energy to move up a gradient

mcat::chem_phys::atomic_structure.stem: Two atoms have the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons. They are:
An electron falling from n=3 to n=2 emits light. A fall from n=4 to n=2 instead emits a photon of:
An element's chemical reactivity is governed mainly by its number of:
Electrons occupy only discrete energy levels rather than any arbitrary energy. This is a direct consequence of:

mcat::chem_phys::periodic_trends.stem: Across a period from left to right, atomic radius generally decreases mainly because:
choices: Effective nuclear charge rises, pulling electrons inward
Shielding grows faster than nuclear charge
mcat::chem_phys::periodic_trends.paraphrase_of: effective nuclear charge shrinks radius across a period

mcat::chem_phys::periodic_trends.stem: Among Na, Mg, Al, and Cl, which has the highest first ionization energy?
mcat::chem_phys::periodic_trends.paraphrase_of: ionization energy rises across a period

mcat::chem_phys::periodic_trends.stem: Which element is the most electronegative?
mcat::chem_phys::periodic_trends.paraphrase_of: electronegativity peaks at the upper right (F)

mcat::chem_phys::periodic_trends.stem: A chloride ion (Cl⁻) compared with a neutral chlorine atom is:
mcat::chem_phys::periodic_trends.paraphrase_of: anions are larger than their parent atoms

mcat::chem_phys::bonding.stem: CO2 has two polar C=O bonds yet is nonpolar overall. This is because:
choices: Its linear geometry cancels the two bond dipoles

mcat::chem_phys::bonding.stem: A molecule AX4 with no lone pairs on the central atom adopts which geometry?
mcat::chem_phys::bonding.paraphrase_of: VSEPR: four bonding pairs give tetrahedral

mcat::chem_phys::bonding.stem: A compound formed from two elements with a very large electronegativity difference (e.g., Na and Cl) is best described as:
mcat::chem_phys::bonding.paraphrase_of: large electronegativity difference gives ionic bonding

mcat::chem_phys::bonding.stem: Water boils at an unusually high temperature for such a small molecule mainly because of:

mcat::chem_phys::stoichiometry.stem: For 2 H2 + O2 → 2 H2O, you mix 4 mol H2 with 1 mol O2. The limiting reactant is:
mcat::chem_phys::stoichiometry.paraphrase_of: identifying the limiting reactant from mole ratio

mcat::chem_phys::stoichiometry.stem: With the other reactant in large excess, doubling the amount of the limiting reactant will:
mcat::chem_phys::stoichiometry.paraphrase_of: product yield scales with limiting reactant

mcat::chem_phys::stoichiometry.stem: For N2 + 3 H2 → 2 NH3, completely reacting 3 mol of H2 (with N2 in excess) produces how many moles of NH3?
mcat::chem_phys::stoichiometry.paraphrase_of: applying mole ratios from a balanced equation

mcat::chem_phys::stoichiometry.stem: A reaction with a theoretical yield of 10 g actually produces 8 g of product. The percent yield is:

mcat::chem_phys::thermodynamics.stem: A reaction is nonspontaneous at low temperature but becomes spontaneous when heated. This implies:
Ice melting at room temperature is spontaneous even though it absorbs heat (ΔH > 0). The reason is that:
choices: The entropy increase (ΔS > 0) makes ΔG negative at that temperature
ΔG is positive but it happens anyway

mcat::chem_phys::thermodynamics.stem: A reaction releases heat to its surroundings. It is:
Which process involves the LARGEST increase in entropy?
choices: A gas condensing to a liquid
A liquid freezing to a solid
A solid subliming directly to a gas
A reaction with no phase change

mcat::chem_phys::kinetics.stem: A catalyst speeds a reaction by:
mcat::chem_phys::kinetics.paraphrase_of: catalysts lower activation energy, not ΔG

mcat::chem_phys::kinetics.stem: A reaction is first order in reactant A. Doubling [A] changes the rate by a factor of:
Raising the temperature speeds up most reactions mainly because it:
choices: Increases the fraction of molecules with enough energy to react
mcat::chem_phys::kinetics.paraphrase_of: temperature raises the fraction above the activation barrier

mcat::chem_phys::kinetics.stem: In a reaction that proceeds through several steps, the overall rate is set by:

mcat::chem_phys::equilibrium.stem: For an exothermic reaction at equilibrium, raising the temperature will:
mcat::chem_phys::equilibrium.paraphrase_of: Le Chatelier: heat as a product of exothermic reaction

mcat::chem_phys::equilibrium.stem: Adding an inert gas at constant VOLUME to a gas-phase equilibrium will:
choices: Shift toward fewer moles of gas
Shift toward more moles of gas
mcat::chem_phys::equilibrium.paraphrase_of: inert gas at constant volume leaves partial pressures unchanged

mcat::chem_phys::equilibrium.stem: Adding more reactant to a system already at equilibrium shifts it:
mcat::chem_phys::equilibrium.paraphrase_of: Le Chatelier: added reactant shifts toward products

mcat::chem_phys::equilibrium.stem: A reaction has an equilibrium constant K = 1×10⁶. At equilibrium the mixture is:

mcat::chem_phys::acids_bases.stem: Titrating a weak acid with strong base, the pH at the half-equivalence point is 4.8. The acid's Ka is closest to:
A buffer must resist pH change near pH 7.2. The best weak-acid component has a pKa of about:
A solution has [H⁺] = 1×10⁻³ M. Its pH is:
For a weak acid HA, its conjugate base is:
mcat::chem_phys::acids_bases.paraphrase_of: conjugate base is the acid minus a proton

mcat::chem_phys::kinematics.stem: A ball is thrown straight up. At the very highest point of its path, its velocity and acceleration are:
mcat::chem_phys::kinematics.paraphrase_of: at the apex v=0 but a=g

mcat::chem_phys::kinematics.stem: Ignoring air resistance, a heavy ball and a light ball dropped from the same height hit the ground:
A ball rolls off a table and becomes a projectile. Ignoring air resistance, during the fall its horizontal velocity:
mcat::chem_phys::kinematics.paraphrase_of: horizontal velocity is constant in projectile motion

mcat::chem_phys::kinematics.stem: A car speeds up from 10 m/s to 20 m/s over 5 s. Its average acceleration is:

mcat::chem_phys::force_energy.stem: A box slides across a floor at constant velocity while friction acts on it. The NET force on the box is:
mcat::chem_phys::force_energy.paraphrase_of: constant velocity means zero net force (Newton's 1st law)

mcat::chem_phys::force_energy.stem: You lift a box 2 m upward, then carry it 5 m horizontally at constant height. Work is done against gravity:
choices: During both the lift and the carry
mcat::chem_phys::force_energy.paraphrase_of: work requires displacement along the force

mcat::chem_phys::force_energy.stem: A book rests on a table. The Newton's-third-law reaction partner to the book pushing down on the table is:
choices: The table pushing up on the book
mcat::chem_phys::force_energy.paraphrase_of: action-reaction pairs act on different objects

mcat::chem_phys::force_energy.stem: If a car doubles its speed, its kinetic energy:

mcat::chem_phys::fluids.stem: An ideal fluid flows through a pipe that narrows. In the narrow section the fluid's speed and pressure, respectively:
mcat::chem_phys::fluids.paraphrase_of: continuity + Bernoulli: faster flow, lower pressure

mcat::chem_phys::fluids.stem: An object floats with 90% of its volume submerged. Its density is about:
In a static fluid, the pressure as you go deeper:
An incompressible fluid flows steadily from a wide pipe into a narrow one. The volume flow rate in the narrow section is:
choices: Larger than in the wide pipe
The same as in the wide pipe
Smaller than in the wide pipe
mcat::chem_phys::fluids.paraphrase_of: continuity: volume flow rate is conserved

mcat::chem_phys::circuits.stem: Two identical resistors are connected in parallel. Their combined resistance, compared with one resistor alone, is:
Adding more resistors in series across a battery of fixed voltage causes the total current to:
mcat::chem_phys::circuits.paraphrase_of: more series resistance lowers current (Ohm's law)

mcat::chem_phys::circuits.stem: A 12 V battery drives current through a single 4 Ω resistor. The current is:
Which expression gives the electrical power dissipated by a resistor carrying current I at voltage V?

mcat::psych_soc::sensation_perception.stem: You stop noticing the feel of your clothes soon after getting dressed. This best illustrates:
You read a smudged word easily once you know the sentence's topic. Using expectations to interpret ambiguous input is:
The minimum stimulus intensity a person can detect 50% of the time is called the:
You can just barely tell a 100 g weight from a 110 g weight. By Weber's law, to just notice a change from a 200 g weight you would need to add about:

mcat::psych_soc::cognition.stem: Told a quiet man who likes poetry is 'either a librarian or a salesman,' a student guesses librarian—ignoring that salesmen are far more numerous. This error reflects:
A person can't solve a puzzle because they fail to see that a box of tacks could also be emptied and used as a shelf. This illustrates:
After heavy news coverage of a shark attack, beachgoers wildly overestimate the risk of one. This overestimate reflects:
mcat::psych_soc::cognition.paraphrase_of: availability heuristic from vivid, recalled examples

mcat::psych_soc::cognition.stem: A researcher notices only the data that support her hypothesis and disregards results that contradict it. This is:

mcat::psych_soc::memory.stem: After learning French, a student struggles to recall Spanish words learned earlier. This is best explained by:
A patient with bilateral hippocampal damage can hold a phone number briefly but cannot form new long-term memories. This dissociation implicates the hippocampus in:
Reviewing material in short sessions spread over two weeks produces better long-term retention than one long cram. This is the:
Shown a long list once, people best recall the first and last items. The strong recall of the LAST few items is the:

mcat::psych_soc::learning.stem: A slot machine pays out unpredictably. Gamblers keep playing at a high, steady rate and resist quitting. This reflects which reinforcement schedule?
A dog salivates to a bell that has repeatedly preceded food. The bell is then rung many times with NO food, and salivation gradually fades. This fading is:
Taking aspirin makes a headache go away, so you become more likely to take aspirin next time. Removing the unpleasant headache is an example of:
mcat::psych_soc::learning.paraphrase_of: negative reinforcement removes an aversive stimulus

mcat::psych_soc::learning.stem: A trainer rewards a dog for closer and closer approximations of rolling over until it performs the full trick. This technique is:

mcat::psych_soc::motivation_emotion.stem: By the Yerkes–Dodson law, performance on a difficult task is best at:
mcat::psych_soc::motivation_emotion.paraphrase_of: Yerkes-Dodson inverted-U of arousal and performance

mcat::psych_soc::motivation_emotion.stem: A theory holds that seeing a bear triggers bodily arousal and the conscious feeling of fear simultaneously and independently, via a thalamic relay. This is:
A theory holds that you feel afraid BECAUSE you notice your heart pounding — the bodily response comes first and the emotion is your reading of it. This is:
By Maslow's hierarchy of needs, a person who is starving is LEAST likely to be motivated at that moment by:

mcat::psych_soc::identity.stem: A teenager tries out different peer groups, values, and career ideas before settling on a sense of self. Erikson would place this in the stage of:
The same person acts solemn at a funeral and boisterous at a ballgame, shifting behavior to fit each setting. This is best described as:
mcat::psych_soc::identity.paraphrase_of: role-based identity varies by social context

mcat::psych_soc::identity.stem: A student gradually thinks of himself as 'the funny one' because peers keep laughing at his jokes and treating him that way. This self-concept built from others' reactions is the:
A medical resident feels torn because being a present parent and being an on-call doctor make incompatible demands at the same time. This is:

mcat::psych_soc::social_thinking.stem: You judge a stranger who trips as 'clumsy' but explain your own trip as 'the floor was wet.' This asymmetry illustrates:
After discussion, a committee of mildly cautious people makes a decision more cautious than any individual started with. This is:
A tight-knit team silences its doubters and makes a bad call because members value harmony over honestly weighing options. This is:
mcat::psych_soc::social_thinking.paraphrase_of: groupthink from a drive for consensus

mcat::psych_soc::social_thinking.stem: A person collapses on a busy sidewalk and no one helps, each pedestrian assuming someone else will. This is:

mcat::psych_soc::social_structure.stem: A hospital has written rules, a hierarchy of doctors and nurses, and an explicit shared purpose of care. Sociologically it is best classified as a(n):
Researchers find health outcomes track people's education, income, and occupation. This pattern is captured by the idea of:
choices: Socioeconomic status as a social determinant of health
mcat::psych_soc::social_structure.paraphrase_of: SES as a social determinant of health

mcat::psych_soc::social_structure.stem: Your immediate family and two lifelong friends, bound by deep and enduring personal ties, form a:
mcat::psych_soc::social_structure.paraphrase_of: primary groups have intimate, enduring ties

mcat::psych_soc::social_structure.stem: A society in which people advance according to demonstrated ability and achievement rather than birth is best described as a:

mcat::psych_soc::demographics.stem: A country's population pyramid is top-heavy: many older adults, few children. A likely consequence is:
choices: Rising demand on pensions and healthcare

mcat::psych_soc::demographics.stem: The steady movement of people from rural areas into cities over time is called:
As a country industrializes, its death rate falls first and its birth rate falls later, producing a population surge that eventually levels off. This pattern is the:
War drives people out of a region while abundant city jobs draw them in. These two forces are known as:
choices: Push and pull factors of migration
mcat::psych_soc::demographics.paraphrase_of: push and pull factors in migration

mcat::psych_soc::biological_behavior.stem: After a stroke, a patient understands speech normally but can no longer produce fluent speech. The damaged region is most likely:
During acute stress the heart races and pupils dilate (fight-or-flight). This response is driven mainly by:
mcat::psych_soc::biological_behavior.paraphrase_of: sympathetic activation in the stress response

mcat::psych_soc::biological_behavior.stem: A drug that raises dopamine activity in the brain's reward pathways is most likely to increase:
Once a stressful event has passed, the branch of the nervous system that slows the heart and promotes 'rest and digest' is the:

mcat::cars.stem: Passage: 'Public monuments do not so much preserve history as freeze a single interpretation of it, discouraging the very reexamination that keeps a society's memory alive.' The author would most likely AGREE that:
choices: Monuments should never be built at all
Reinterpreting the past has value, and monuments can impede it
History has exactly one correct interpretation
A society's memory does not matter

mcat::cars.stem: Passage: 'Critics blame the program's failure on underfunding. Yet comparable programs with far larger budgets fared no better, suggesting the flaw lay in the design, not the money.' The second sentence primarily functions to:
choices: Offer evidence that challenges the critics' explanation
Concede that the critics are correct
mcat::cars.paraphrase_of: role of a sentence within an argument (CARS)

mcat::cars.stem: Passage: 'The committee's so-called reforms, trumpeted in every press release, amounted to little more than rearranging the deck chairs.' The author's attitude toward the reforms is best described as:
Passage: 'Because the new drug reduced symptoms in the trial, it should be approved for use nationwide.' This argument most depends on the unstated assumption that:
choices: The trial's results generalize to the wider population
The drug is inexpensive to make

mcat::cars.stem: Passage: 'Sociologists once cast the modern city as an engine of anomie, dissolving the tight bonds of village life. Yet recent ethnographies uncover dense webs of mutual aid in urban neighborhoods—ties that are simply harder for outsiders to see than a crowded village square.' The primary purpose of the passage is to:
choices: Argue that villages will always sustain stronger bonds than cities
Claim that modern city neighborhoods are free of social problems
Revise an older view by arguing that urban social bonds exist but are less visible
Recommend that sociologists stop studying rural villages
mcat::cars.paraphrase_of: identifying the primary purpose of a passage (CARS)

mcat::cars.stem: Passage: 'The flattened space of early medieval painting was no lapse in skill but a deliberate choice: artists muted worldly depth to foreground spiritual hierarchy, scaling figures by holiness rather than by distance.' Which finding, if true, would most STRENGTHEN the author's claim?
choices: Medieval pigments were costly and difficult to obtain
Renaissance painters a century later perfected linear perspective
Most surviving medieval panels depict non-religious subjects
The same artists rendered convincing depth in their secular sketchbooks
mcat::cars.paraphrase_of: recognizing evidence that strengthens an argument (CARS)

mcat::cars.stem: Passage: 'The germ theory's history is usually told as a triumph of reason: once Pasteur produced decisive evidence, the medical establishment promptly abandoned belief in spontaneous generation.' Which of the following, if true, would most WEAKEN the author's characterization?
choices: Prominent physicians resisted germ theory for over two decades, relenting only as a new generation replaced them
Pasteur was later awarded numerous scientific honors
Germ theory is today accepted by essentially all scientists
Belief in spontaneous generation dated back to Aristotle
mcat::cars.paraphrase_of: recognizing evidence that weakens an argument (CARS)

mcat::cars.stem: Passage: 'To judge an act courageous we must know what the person feared and why they pressed on regardless; the very same outward deed may be reckless in one agent and brave in another.' The author would most likely AGREE that:
choices: An act's visible consequences alone determine whether it was brave
Assessing a virtue requires weighing the agent's motives and fears, not just the deed
Courage and recklessness are ultimately indistinguishable
Feeling fear disqualifies an act from being courageous
mcat::cars.paraphrase_of: inferring what an author would agree with (CARS)

mcat::cars.stem: Passage: 'A representative serves the public good only by voting exactly as a majority of constituents instructs; to do otherwise is to substitute one's private judgment for the will of the people.' The argument most depends on the unstated assumption that:
choices: Constituents never disagree with one another
Legislators are less educated than the people they represent
The majority's expressed preference reliably tracks the public good
Representatives should be limited to a single term

mcat::cars.stem: Passage: 'Her debut novel has been praised as fearlessly experimental. In truth, its scrambled timelines and vanishing narrators feel less like daring than like a writer hiding a thin plot behind a curtain of tricks.' The reviewer's attitude toward the novel is best described as:
choices: Dismissive of its experimentation as a cover for weak substance

mcat::cars.stem: Passage: 'Gift-giving looks like pure generosity, yet anthropologists note that a lavish gift can quietly bind the receiver to repay. In this light the gift is not merely kindness—it is also a claim on the future.' The phrase 'a claim on the future' primarily functions to:
choices: Reframe apparent generosity as also creating an obligation to reciprocate
Prove that every gift is fundamentally selfish
Introduce an unrelated theory of market pricing
Concede that gift-giving serves no social role at all
mcat::cars.paraphrase_of: the function of a phrase within an argument (CARS)

mcat::cars.stem: Passage: 'A promise binds only because both parties share the expectation that such words will be kept; strip away that shared expectation and the words are merely sounds.' Applying the author's reasoning, a promise spoken to a listener who does not understand the language would:
choices: Remain fully binding, because the words were still uttered
Not be binding, because no shared expectation of keeping it was created
Become binding only once it is put in writing
Show that promises never generate any obligation
mcat::cars.paraphrase_of: applying an author's logic to a new situation (CARS)

mcat::cars.stem: Passage: 'Towns that opened public libraries in the 1890s later recorded higher literacy than towns without them. Clearly, building the libraries raised literacy.' Which finding, if true, would most WEAKEN this causal conclusion?
choices: The new libraries stocked mostly popular fiction
Literacy is widely regarded as socially valuable
Several of the library buildings were architecturally grand
The towns that built libraries were already wealthier and better schooled beforehand
mcat::cars.paraphrase_of: spotting a confound that weakens a causal claim (CARS)

mcat::cars.stem: Passage: 'We love to praise musical originality, yet every composer works within inherited scales, forms, and expectations. What we hear as new is really a fresh rearrangement of the familiar, not creation from nothing.' The central thesis of the passage is that:
choices: Genuine originality in music is impossible for listeners to perceive
Composers ought to abandon traditional forms entirely
Musical originality is a novel recombination of inherited materials rather than creation from nothing
Shared scales inevitably make all music sound alike
mcat::cars.paraphrase_of: identifying the central thesis of a passage (CARS)
